nodes.closure_condition: Prove a lower bound N ≫ 2^n (no 1/√n loss) for any sum-distinct (distinct-subset-sum / dissociated) set A ⊆ {1,...,N} with |A|=n; equivalently F(x) < log_2 x + O(1) for the max dissociated set in [1,x]. The bottleneck is a Littlewood–Offord / second-moment-of-subset-sums argument that gains the missing √n factor over the current best N ≥ C(n, ⌊n/2⌋) ≈ √(2/π)·2^n/√n (Dubroff–Fox–Xu, Elkies–Gleason constant). NOT finitely closeable (infinite family, n→∞).

nodes.label: The set of natural numbers that can be written as a sum of a prime and at most $k$ powers of $2$.
nodes.closure_condition: Decide whether a finite k exists with every large n = prime + (at most k powers of 2). The precise missing input is either (a) a covering-system / congruence obstruction (built from the order of 2 mod p^2, per Granville-Soundararajan) producing a positive-density set of integers avoiding prime + k powers of 2 for ALL k, which would prove NO such k exists; or (b) a sieve/density upgrade pushing Gallagher's lower-density-(1-eps) result to FULL density (empty exceptional set) for some fixed k, covering every large integer. Cannot be closed by finite computation (site-stated). A finite witness CAN only refute specific small-k variants (e.g. Grechuk: 1117175146 not prime+3 powers), not the main k-existence question.

nodes.closure_condition: Remove the log-factor in the diameter lower bound: prove diam(A) >> n (linear) for any n-point planar set with pairwise distances >= 1 and distinct distances separated by >= 1. The bottleneck is improving the current best bound diam >> n/log n (inherited from the Guth-Katz distinct-distances theorem, which gives ~ n/log n distinct distances) up to the conjectured linear bound diam >= n - 1. Closing requires either a distinct-distances lower bound free of the log loss in this separated-distance regime, or a direct geometric argument exploiting the unit-separation of distinct distance values.

nodes.closure_condition: The problem asks: for every k>=2 does there exist a prime p and k consecutive intervals each with product ==1 (mod p). Via the Adenwalla/Luccioli reduction (Lean-formalized) this is EQUIVALENT to the Noll-Simmons factorial form: solutions to q_1! == ... == q_k! (mod p) with arbitrarily large k. Known cases: k=2 (Erdos, mod 11), k=3 (Makowski, mod 17), and (June 2026, octavioalberto/Hernandez Santiago) k=4 for infinitely many primes via Wilson's-theorem pairing (q==1 mod 6, p | q!-1, tuple (1,q,p-1-q,p-2)). The SINGLE missing input that would CLOSE the problem is: a construction/lower-bound guaranteeing arbitrarily many simultaneous solutions n in [1,p-1] to n! == c (mod p) for a common residue c (equivalently, that the equivalence classes of the partial-factorial sequence {n! mod p} can be made arbitrarily large for suitable p). Concretely, one needs to extend the Hardy-Subbarao/Wilson congruence + covering-system trick (which forces the intermediate values q_i+2, 2q_i+1, etc. composite so that pairing n! and (p-1-n)! stays valid) from producing a BOUNDED count (=4) of congruent factorials per prime to producing an UNBOUNDED count k -> infinity. Absent that, the answer for large k is unknown. A clean closing object: an infinite family of primes p each admitting k(p) -> infinity values q with q! lying in one fixed residue class mod p, with the necessary primality/compositeness exclusions handled by an all-suitable-moduli covering / arithmetic-progression argument.

If defined, the deficiency is the count of $0 \le i < k$ such that $n - i$ is $k$-smooth.
nodes.closure_condition: Two independent sub-questions about the "deficiency" of a binomial coefficient C(n,k) (n>=2k, no prime p<=k dividing it; deficiency = #{0<=i<k : n-i is k-smooth}). PART (i): are there infinitely many C(n,k) with deficiency exactly 1? — no machinery exists; would need a construction/density argument producing an infinite family where exactly one of the k consecutive integers in (n-k,n] is k-smooth while the rest are prime-rich, the harder and still-totally-open half. PART (ii): are there only finitely many with deficiency >1? — the precise missing input is supplied by Barreto's forum reduction: it follows from (A) a strengthened xyz_fin-conjecture of Lagarias–Soundararajan — a kappa_0>1 such that there are only finitely many primitive triples X+Y=Z whose largest prime factor S(X,Y,Z) < (log H)^{kappa_0 - eps} — PLUS (B) a 'height-forcing' lower bound log h_2(k) >> k^alpha with alpha>1/kappa_0 (h_2(k) = least n with deficiency>=2). The existing unconditional ELS93 bound n << 2^k sqrt(k) is only an upper bound and is insufficient. So the single most-specific bottleneck for the (finiteness) half is: prove an abc/xyz-strength smooth-triple bound (Conjecture A) together with the super-polynomial height-forcing bound (Conjecture B). Neither part is closed unconditionally.
nodes.prior_art: full solve blocked by subset-product CRT mixing + second-slot pair-sieve; use as support for #1094

nodes.label: For all $n\ge 2k$ the least prime factor of $\binom{n}{k}$ is $\le\max(n/k,k)$, with only finitely many exceptions.
nodes.closure_condition: A uniform-in-k proof that the set S = {(n,k) : 2k ≤ n, minFac(C(n,k)) > max(n/k, k)} is finite — equivalently, an effective theorem that for all but finitely many (n,k) with n/k < k (the hard regime, where one needs a prime factor ≤ k), the binomial C(n,k) is divisible by some prime p ≤ k. The bottleneck is the deficiency ≥ 1 case (link to [1093]): counterexamples can only come from binomials where no prime in the window controls the factorization to first order, so the precise missing input is a finiteness/structure theorem bounding the (n,k) for which every prime factor of C(n,k) exceeds max(n/k, k). Erdős already has lpf ≤ n/k for n large *depending on k*; the gap is removing the dependence on k (uniformity) and certifying that the ELS88 exception list (the 14 listed binomials) is exactly complete. Because the problem cannot be resolved by finite computation, closure requires an analytic argument (Sylvester–Erdős / consecutive-integer prime-divisor bounds made uniform), not enumeration.
nodes.prior_art: positive-deficiency prime-cofactor lemma (outside ELS exceptions, some nonsmooth cofactor has least prime factor <= max(N/k,k))

nodes.label: Let $g(k)>k+1$ be the smallest $n$ such that all prime factors of $\binom{n}{k}$ are $>k$.
nodes.closure_condition: Prove a lower bound g(k) >= exp(c·k/log k) for the Erdős-Selfridge function g(k) = least n>k+1 with every prime factor of C(n,k) exceeding k (the ELS93 'right-thinking person' conjecture). The current record is only g(k) >> exp(c(log k)^2) (Konyagin), exponentially short of the conjectured truth. The bottleneck input is: a Kummer-carry / covering-density argument showing that for every n below exp(c·k/log k) there is some prime p<=k whose base-p digits of n force a carry in n choose k (so p | C(n,k)); equivalently, no integer n in that exponentially-large window can simultaneously dodge all primes p<=k via the carry condition. Combined with the existing EES74 upper bound exp((1+o(1))k), the matching lower bound exp(Theta(k/log k)) (SSW20 heuristic) would close the estimate of log g(k). No finite computation can settle it.

This is exactly the existence direction of the Filaseta–Finch–Kozek dichotomy: prove every Sierpinski number is EITHER a perfect power OR has a finite covering set (the FFK08 conjecture), with the perfect-power branch supplying a non-covering Sierpinski witness. The bottleneck is a rigorous 'no finite prime set covers all n' lower-bound argument for this one perfect 4th power — heuristically argued by FFK08 but not proven, since ruling out ALL finite covering sets is not a finite computation. A negative answer (no such non-covering Sierpinski number exists) would imply infinitely many Fermat primes, so a clean closure either way is blocked by the same wall as the Fermat-prime finiteness question.

nodes.label: Let $k\geq 3$. Can the product of any $k$ consecutive integers $N$ ever be powerful? That is, must there always exist a prime $p\mid N$ such
nodes.closure_condition: Prove the Erdős–Selfridge conjecture: for every k≥3 and every n, the product N=(n+1)···(n+k) of k consecutive integers is NOT powerful — equivalently, exhibit at least one prime p|N with p²∤N. The precise missing input is a uniform-over-the-window guarantee that the square-full part cannot absorb all k factors at once: an unconditional lower bound forcing some prime to divide exactly one factor (n+i) to the first power only. This is strictly stronger than the proven ES75 'never a perfect power' result (powerful ⊋ perfect-power) and must distinguish k≥3 from k=2 (where n(n+1) IS powerful infinitely often via Pell, problem #364). The cleanest sufficient lemma is Erdős's own sharpening (the multiple_powerful_factors variant): for fixed k and all large n, every product m(m+1)···(m+n) has ≥k distinct primes p with p|N, p²∤N — provable would require an effective count of first-power (square-free-part) primes per consecutive-integer window. No abc-reduction or covering-system argument is known to deliver this.

So the precise bottleneck is: show that no maximum Sidon set on {1,...,N+k} can carry >= 2 'removable' elements at BOTH the bottom window {1,...,k} and the top window {N+1,...,N+k}, i.e. effectively force the relevant g-quantity down to 1 in the large-N regime. Refuting it requires constructing, for some fixed k and infinitely many N, a Sidon set of {1,...,N+k} that beats every Sidon set of {1,...,N} by >= 2. No finite computation can resolve either direction.

nodes.closure_condition: SOLVED. The Erdős large-prime-gap conjecture (for every C>0 infinitely many n with p_{n+1}-p_n > C·(loglog n · logloglog log n)/(loglog log n)^2 · log n) was proved in the affirmative independently by Maynard [Ma16] and by Ford-Green-Konyagin-Tao [FGKT16] (2016), strengthening Rankin's [Ra38] fixed-constant bound. The current best lower bound, due to all five authors [FGKMT18], removes the (loglog log n)^2 denominator: p_{n+1}-p_n ≫ (loglog n · logloglog log n / loglog log n)·log n. The remaining (open, not part of #4's resolved claim) targets are Erdős's $5000/$10000 problems: a lower bound > (log n)^{1+c} for some c>0, with conjectured truth ≫ (log n)^2. The Lean theorems erdos_4 and erdos_4.variants.rankin are stated but carry `sorry` (formal proof not yet supplied); closing the formalization gap requires a Lean proof of the FGKT/Maynard construction (sieve weights + GPY/Maynard machinery + Westzynthius-type covering of a long composite interval).

This is governed by Erdős's companion conjecture F(n) = min_{m>n}(m - p(m)) with n - F(n) ~ c·n^{1/2}, which depends on counting semiprimes p·q with p,q ≈ sqrt(m) in short intervals and the spacing of consecutive such m — itself dependent on prime-distribution-in-short-intervals / Selberg-type sieve estimates that are conditional on 'plausible conjectures on primes' (as Erdős-Eggleton-Selfridge note for the twin problem #385). nodes.closure_condition: The missing input is an obstruction lemma for degree >= 3 that replaces the quadratic difference-set argument. Concretely: prove that for every polynomial f in Z[X] of degree >= 3 (smallest open case f = X^3), no set A subset Z can be a perfect additive complement of the value set {f(n)} — i.e. Z cannot be tiled exactly-once by A (+) f(Z). The degree-2 case is SOLVED because f(Z)-f(Z) contains every multiple of a fixed q (q = 2b if b != 0, q = 4a if b = 0), so any (necessarily infinite) A has A-A hitting a multiple of q, yielding a forced double representation n = a+f(k) = b+f(-k). For deg >= 3 the difference set f(Z)-f(Z) is sparse and is NOT a union of full arithmetic progressions, so it contains the multiples of no fixed modulus; the pigeonhole on A-A collapses. The precise bottleneck object is therefore: a structural impossibility certificate for exact additive tilings of Z by a sparse polynomial value set of degree >= 3 — e.g. a counting/density contradiction (the value set {f(n) : |n| <= N} has size ~N while a perfect complement would need ~ X/N elements of A in [0,X], and exact-once representation must hold for ALL n, a condition the sparse non-AP difference structure should preclude). Equivalently, prove the cubic (X^3) variant erdos_477.variants.X_pow_three. There is no candidate construction believed to exist (owner and Erdős/Graham expect the answer NO), so closure = a proof of universal non-tileability for deg >= 3, not a search for an example.

nodes.closure_condition: SUFFICIENT (and the website's intended route): prove that there are infinitely many primes p such that 8p^2 - 1 is also prime. This single prime-pair statement closes the problem: for each such p, n = 8p^2 - 1 with n+1 = 2^3·p^2 gives n(n+1) = (8p^2-1)·2^3·p^2 with exponent multiset {1,3,2}, all distinct. This is exactly the missing input — the Lean formalization already PROVES the conditional implication erdos_913.variants.conditional: {p | p.Prime ∧ (8p^2-1).Prime}.Infinite → (the Erdős 913 set is infinite), so the ONLY unproven lemma is the infinitude of primes p with 8p^2-1 prime. This is a special case of Schinzel's Hypothesis H / the Bateman–Horn conjecture applied to the irreducible polynomial f(p)=8p^2-1 (admissible: no fixed prime divisor); it is far beyond current prime-distribution technology (parabola/quadratic prime values, an analogue of the n^2+1 problem). NOTE: this prime-pair input is sufficient but not necessary — any one of several standard conjectures suffices (infinitely many Mersenne primes 2^q-1 also works, giving n = 2^q-1, n+1 = 2^q with exponents {1,q}; and more generally Hypothesis H for 2^a·p^b - 1 with gcd(a,b)=1 gives exponents {1,a,b}). So the minimal closure object is: a proof of infinitely many prime values of the quadratic 8p^2-1 over prime arguments p (or any single one of these admissible polynomial-prime conjectures).

nodes.closure_condition: Prove (or disprove) that for all sufficiently large n, every diameter-minimizing planar n-point set with minimum distance 1 contains a unit equilateral triangle. The precise bottleneck is a structural rigidity lemma forcing optimal configurations to coincide with the triangular lattice: specifically, show that any diameter-minimizer must have (1-o(1))n points lying on the triangular (hexagonal) lattice intersected with a disc — Erdős's stronger conjecture — which would force a unit equilateral triangle. Equivalently, a counterexample is an infinite family of diameter-optimal n-point min-distance-1 sets containing NO unit equilateral triangle. Owner notes Sendov and Simonovits doubted the conjecture, so direction itself is open. Statement is false for small n (n=4 square). Explicitly NOT finitely decidable: an 'eventually for large n' statement over a continuous configuration space.
